paragraphs.text:
## 1) What amino acids already have Zn²⁺ stability data in SRD-46?

From the Zn²⁺ (`metal_208`) stability search over canonical amino-acid ligands, these ligands had retrieved Zn²⁺ records:

| Ligand | Ligand ID | Zn²⁺ stability data retrieved? | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Glycine | ligand_5760 | Yes | N,O donor |
| Alanine | ligand_5761 | Yes | N,O donor |
| Aspartic acid | ligand_5802 | Yes | extra carboxylate |
| Glutamic acid | ligand_5804 | Yes | extra carboxylate |
| Tyrosine | ligand_5819 | Yes | phenol side chain |
| Serine | ligand_5828 | Yes | hydroxyl side chain |
| Threonine | ligand_5829 | Yes | hydroxyl side chain |
| Asparagine | ligand_5843 | Yes | amide side chain |
| Glutamine | ligand_5844 | Yes | amide side chain |
| Cysteine | ligand_5856 | Yes | thiol/thiolate S donor |
| Histidine | ligand_5898 | Yes | imidazole N donor |

So the “not yet studied” pool is most plausibly among the missing canonical residues such as **methionine, valine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, proline, lysine, arginine, tryptophan**, etc., at least within the data retrieved here.

paragraphs.text:
## 2) What do the studied Zn²⁺ amino-acid systems imply?

The retrieved Zn²⁺ dataset shows a clear donor-type trend:

| Ligand | Donor character | Zn²⁺ dataset richness |
|---|---|---|
| Glycine | simple N,O | moderate |
| Alanine | simple N,O | moderate |
| Aspartic acid / Glutamic acid | harder O-rich | present, but not unusually rich |
| Serine / Threonine / Tyrosine | OH/phenol side chains | present, limited |
| Asparagine / Glutamine | amide side chains | present, limited |
| Histidine | imidazole N + N,O | strong/chemically favorable |
| Cysteine | thiolate S + N,O | richest/most favorable-looking among canonical amino acids retrieved |

This is chemically consistent with Zn²⁺ favoring **borderline-soft donor atoms**, especially **S** and good **N** donors, more than purely hard O-donor side chains.

claims.text: ## 2) What do the studied Zn²⁺ amino-acid systems imply?
The retrieved Zn²⁺ dataset shows a clear donor-type trend:
| Glycine | simple N,O | moderate |
| Alanine | simple N,O | moderate |
| Aspartic acid / Glutamic acid | harder O-rich | present, but not unusually rich |
| Serine / Threonine / Tyrosine | OH/phenol side chains | present, limited |
| Asparagine / Glutamine | amide side chains | present, limited |
| Histidine | imidazole N + N,O | strong/chemically favorable |
| Cysteine | thiolate S + N,O | richest/most favorable-looking among canonical amino acids retrieved |
This is chemically consistent with Zn²⁺ favoring **borderline-soft donor atoms**,
especially **S** and good **N** donors,
more than purely hard O-donor side chains.

paragraphs.text:
## 3) What do the pKa data say about donor availability?

Representative pKa values from SRD-46 at 25 °C, I = 0.1 M:

| Ligand | Key pKa values retrieved | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Glycine | 9.57 | mostly α-NH3⁺ deprotonation in upper range; no special side-chain donor |
| Alanine | 9.71 | similar to glycine |
| Aspartic acid | 1.95, 3.71, 9.66 | extra carboxylate available, but O-donor dominated |
| Glutamic acid | 2.16, 4.15, 9.58 | similar O-donor behavior |
| Histidine | 6.05, 9.10 | imidazole donor becomes important near neutral pH |
| Cysteine | 8.18, 10.3 (plus low-pKa protonation entry) | thiol/thiolate chemistry strongly relevant for Zn²⁺ |
| Tyrosine | 9.04, 10.1 | phenol only deprotonates at relatively high pH |
| Serine / Threonine | ~2.2 and ~9 | side-chain alcohol is weak donor |
| Asparagine / Glutamine | ~2.2 and ~8.7–9.0 | side-chain amides are weak neutral donors |

The key pattern is that **histidine** and especially **cysteine** have side-chain donor groups that can materially strengthen Zn²⁺ binding, whereas hydroxy, amide, and aliphatic side chains do not.

from SRD-46
| Glycine | 9.57 | mostly α-NH3⁺ deprotonation in upper range; no special side-chain donor |
| Alanine | 9.71 | similar to glycine |
| Aspartic acid | 1.95, 3.71, 9.66 | extra carboxylate available, but O-donor dominated |
| Glutamic acid | 2.16, 4.15, 9.58 | similar O-donor behavior |
| Histidine | 6.05, 9.10 | imidazole donor becomes important near neutral pH |
| Cysteine | 8.18, 10.3 (plus low-pKa protonation entry) | thiol/thiolate chemistry strongly relevant for Zn²⁺ |
| Tyrosine | 9.04, 10.1 | phenol only deprotonates at relatively high pH |
| Serine / Threonine | ~2.2 and ~9 | side-chain alcohol is weak donor |
| Asparagine / Glutamine | ~2.2 and ~8.7–9.0 | side-chain amides are weak neutral donors |
The key pattern is that
**histidine** and especially **cysteine** have side-chain donor groups that can materially strengthen Zn²⁺ binding
hydroxy, amide, and aliphatic side chains do not.

paragraphs.text:
## 4) Why methionine is the best unstudied prediction

Among canonical amino acids apparently lacking Zn²⁺ data in the retrieved set, **methionine** is the most plausible strongest binder because it is the only common missing residue with a **sulfur-containing side chain**.

| Unstudied candidate type | Expected Zn²⁺ affinity |
|---|---|
| Valine / Leucine / Isoleucine / Phenylalanine | mostly simple α-amino acid behavior; no strong extra donor |
| Proline | N,O chelation possible, but no especially strong side-chain donor |
| Lysine / Arginine | side chains are strongly protonated in water; poor Zn donor advantage near neutral pH |
| Tryptophan | indole is not a strong coordinating donor in water |
| **Methionine** | adds a **thioether sulfur**, which should enhance Zn²⁺ affinity over O/N-only amino acids |

The strongest evidence chain from SRD-46 is:

| Observation from retrieved data | Implication |
|---|---|
| Glycine/alanine are ordinary Zn²⁺ binders | simple N,O donors are baseline |
| Histidine is stronger-looking because of imidazole N | extra side-chain donor matters a lot |
| Cysteine is the standout sulfur-containing canonical ligand with rich Zn²⁺ complexation | sulfur donors are especially favorable for Zn²⁺ |
| Methionine is the obvious missing canonical sulfur amino acid | likely strongest among the unstudied residues |

claims.text: ## 4) Why methionine is the best unstudied prediction
Among canonical amino acids apparently lacking Zn²⁺ data in the retrieved set,
**methionine** is the most plausible strongest binder
because
it is the only common missing residue with a **sulfur-containing side chain**
| Valine / Leucine / Isoleucine / Phenylalanine | mostly simple α-amino acid behavior; no strong extra donor |
| Proline | N,O chelation possible, but no especially strong side-chain donor |
| Lysine / Arginine | side chains are strongly protonated in water; poor Zn donor advantage near neutral pH |
| Tryptophan | indole is not a strong coordinating donor in water |
| **Methionine** | adds a **thioether sulfur**, which should enhance Zn²⁺ affinity over O/N-only amino acids |
The strongest evidence chain from SRD-46 is:
| Glycine/alanine are ordinary Zn²⁺ binders | simple N,O donors are baseline |
| Histidine is stronger-looking because of imidazole N | extra side-chain donor matters a lot |
| Cysteine is the standout sulfur-containing canonical ligand with rich Zn²⁺ complexation | sulfur donors are especially favorable for Zn²⁺ |
| Methionine is the obvious missing canonical sulfur amino acid | likely strongest among the unstudied residues |

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Proline can N,O chelate but lacks a particularly strong side-chain donor toward Zn(II) in water.
Lys and Arg side chains are protonated in water near neutral pH, making them poor donors to Zn(II).
Tryptophan’s indole is a weak donor to Zn(II) in aqueous solution.
Thioether sulfur (as in methionine) is typically a better Zn(II) donor than O/N-only side chains in water.

## 5) Final prediction

| Predicted amino acid | Why |
|---|---|
| **Methionine** | It is the strongest plausible **unstudied canonical amino acid** because the retrieved Zn²⁺ trend favors side-chain donor atoms beyond simple N,O chelation, and sulfur-containing amino acids are the most favorable class in the observed dataset. Methionine’s thioether sulfur should give it an advantage over aliphatic, hydroxy, amide, aromatic, or strongly protonated basic side chains. |

paragraphs.text:
## Bottom line

**Prediction: methionine**.

The reasoning is that SRD-46 shows Zn²⁺ complexes become notably stronger when amino acids offer **side-chain donor groups**, with **histidine (imidazole N)** and especially **cysteine (sulfur)** standing out over simple amino acids. Among the apparently unstudied canonical amino acids, **methionine is the best sulfur-containing candidate**, so it is the most likely to form the most stable Zn²⁺ complex.

*Source: NIST SRD-46 retrieved Zn²⁺ stability and ligand pKa records.*
